coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated copper oxide for the sensitive and selective detection of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug in berries
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Muthusankar, Ganesan
Sasikumar, Ragu
Chen, Shen-Ming
Gopu, Gopalakrishnan
Sengottuvelan, Nallathambi
Rwei, Syang-Peng
coredata.dc:description: We report a sensitive non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug aspirin (ASA) sensor studies using the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) decorated copper oxide (Cu2O). A simplistic approach of electrochemical deposition method has been used to prepare the N-CQD incorporated with copper oxide (N-CQD/Cu2O) and the resulting composite has characterized by analytical techniques. Modified glassy carbon electrode of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE is developed and is used for the sensor studies of aspirin. The modified N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE has exhibited a higher current (Ipa) response to the oxidation process when compared to N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE and bare GCE. Furthermore, it has shown a good linear range of 1–907 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) ∼0.002 µM and sensitivity ∼21.87 µA µM−1 cm-2. The developed sensor has displayed outstanding repeatability, stability and accumulation time along with better electro-catalytic response in berries for real-life application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Copper oxide
Electrochemical deposition
Aspirin
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of ASA at different modified electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank for the financial support of the UGC – BSR (25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7 – 88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15 th -Oct-2015) fellowship, India and The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 106-2113-M-027-003). We would like to thank the University Science Instrumentation Centre (USIC), Alagappa University, Karaikudi, India for the XRD facilities. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrodeposition of N-CQD/Cu2O on GCE in 4.5 mL 0.1 M NaOH solution contains 0.5 mL N-CQD. CV curves were recorded for 40 consecutive cycles between the potential ranges of −0.5 to +0.3 V at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1.
(A) XRD pattern and (B) FT-IR for N-CQD/Cu2O composite.
FE-SEM images of (A-C) Cu MCs, (D-F) Cu2O and (G-I) N-CQD/Cu2O composite at different magnifications.
(A) EIS plots of 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl recorded at different electrodes of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE and N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE modified GCE and (B) Randles equivalent circuit model.
CV response of bare GCE, N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE and N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE with 200 µM ASA in 0.1 M NaOH.
CVs of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE (A) Different concentrations of ASA (a = absence; b-f = 100–500 µM ASA) and (B) plot of anodic peak current versus the concentration of ASA.
(A) Different scanning rates of 200 µM ASA from 20 to 200 mV s−1 and (B) plot of anodic peak current versus the square root of the scan rates.
DPVs of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE (A) increasing ASA concentration (a to o = 1–907 µM) and (B) calibration curve of the peak currents on the ASA concentrations. Scanning rate: 50 mV s−1 .
Variation in DPV current response of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE for ASA in the presence of.

CVs found at (A) accumulation time, (B) reproducibility, (C) operational stability for 50 nonstop cycles and (D) Storage stability of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE containing 200 µM of ASA. Scanning rate: 50 mV s−1.

DPVs of the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE in (A, D) Rubus (blackberry), (B, E) Rubus idaeus (raspberry) and (C, F) Oxycoccus (cranberry) fruit samples. The fruit samples containing ASA was added consecutively to 0.1 M NaOH at the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1.
Schematic illustration of the electrochemical preparation of N-CQD/Cu2O composite and sensitive detection of ASA in berries.
Overall oxidation mechanism of ASA at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated copper oxide for the sensitive and selective detection of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug in berries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630003, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Organic and Polymeric Materials, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei 106, Taiwan, ROC

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Organic and Polymeric Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a sensitive non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug aspirin (ASA) sensor studies using the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) decorated copper oxide (Cu2O). A simplistic approach of electrochemical deposition method has been used to prepare the N-CQD incorporated with copper oxide (N-CQD/Cu2O) and the resulting composite has characterized by analytical techniques. Modified glassy carbon electrode of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE is developed and is used for the sensor studies of aspirin. The modified N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE has exhibited a higher current (Ipa) response to the oxidation process when compared to N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE and bare GCE. Furthermore, it has shown a good linear range of 1–907 µM with a limit of detection (LOD) ∼0.002 µM and sensitivity ∼21.87 µA µM−1 cm-2. The developed sensor has displayed outstanding repeatability, stability and accumulation time along with better electro-catalytic response in berries for real-life application.

ce:section.ce:para: A new class of low-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials including fullerenes [1], carbon nanotubes [2], graphene [3], graphitic carbon nitride [4], carbon quantum dots [5], carbon nanodiamonds [6], carbon nanocones [7], carbon nanodisks [8] and carbon nanothreads [9] have a great deal of research interest in the recent years. This is because of their small size, unique electrical, optical, catalytic properties with the large surface area that helps them to exhibit tremendous applications in the research fields like catalysts, energy storage devices and sensors.

Among these, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) gain more attention than the other carbonaceous materials due to its low-cost preparation, easily soluble in water, rich physio-chemical properties, photoinduced electron-transfer, redox behavior, superior luminescent and biocompatibility [10,11]. Typically CQDs are sp2 hybridized graphitic nanocrystalline material. The unfulfilled valences over the surface of the carbon atoms end up with loosely swinging bonds which will be inherent or self-functionalized with a number of functional groups. Moreover, the functional groups (carboxylic, carbonyl and alcoholic functional groups) on the surface of the CQDs lead variation in its surface and diameter in order to resemble the quantum confinement effects [12]. Owing to these uniqueness, CQDs have been prompted in various fields such as photovoltaics, energy storage devices, bioimaging, sensors and photoinduced catalysis [13,14]. However, the usage of the CQDs as an electrochemical sensor for the biological samples is still in the exploring stage. Doping of the CQDs play an essential role in altering the electron transfer behavior as well as the optical property of the CQDs [32]. Among the other heteroatoms, nitrogen doping is the most favorable approach to enhance the CQDs property. This may be due to the similarities in atomic size between nitrogen and carbon atoms [33]. Nitrogen doping into the CQDs alters the interior electronic environment which exhibited outstanding performance in the bio-sensing field [34]. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQD) are prepared from the citric acid as a carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source by greener route, a microwave-assisted method.

So far, carbon-based materials and its heterostructured metal oxides with unique structures have been developed and used in various applications such as batteries, supercapacitors, solar cells, electrocatalysts and sensors. [15–21]. Balogun et al. have designed CQDs modified VO2 nanowires as a cathode material for lithium and sodium ion batteries [15]. Li et al. synthesized the NiMoO4/reduced graphene oxide composite material for the high-performance supercapacitor application [16]. Ojha et al. analyzed the reduced graphene oxide decorated with CeO2 doped MnO2 nanorods for the supercapacitor appliance [17]. Zheng et al. have reported the reduced graphene oxide with metal oxide as cathode material for the organic solar cells [18]. Tian et al. developed the metal free graphitic carbon nitride/ZnO nanosheets for hydrogen peroxide sensor [19], and also reported that the platinum doped metal oxide nanorod arrays over the carbon nitride nanosheets for gas sensor application [20]. Hameed et al. prepared the NiO nanoparticles on graphene nanosheets for the electro-oxidation of urea [21]. From the above research works, it can be identified that the combined structure of low-dimensional carbon material with metal oxide can offer better performance than the individual constituents. In addition to that carbon component also improves the conductivity and the stability of the metal oxide.

Amongst all the metal oxides, cuprous oxide (Cu2O) is reported as a potential p-type semiconductor for an electrochemical sensor of various drugs, organic molecules, and environmental pollutants. The aptness is owing to the intrinsic properties of a cuprous oxide, such as redox stability, low charge transfer, exclusive sensitivity and selectivity [22–24]. However, less stability of the Cu2O reduces its variety of application. It was reported in the literature that the existence of carbon layer can improve the stability of the Cu2O [25]. Hence, it is evident that the carbon-based materials can improve the stability of Cu2O and also no need for the demanding synthesis process. With this reference, we have prepared the CQDs decorated Cu2O with a simplistic approach of electro-deposition method to improve the stability.

Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) medication is prescribed to the worldwide due to the versatile effect over the back pain, osteoarthritis joint pain, rheumatoid arthritis, fever, migraine, postoperative pain and so on [26]. The common types of NSAIDs are Aspirin (ASA), Ibuprofen, Naproxen, and celecoxib. Among all, ASA is the most well-known to the medication of pain, Kawasaki diseases, heart attacks, ischaemic strokes, colorectal cancer and helps to prevent blood clots [27]. Additionally, ASA is readily available in fruits such as apple, raspberry, cranberry, strawberry, kiwi, and broccoli. The number of methods is proposed for the determination of ASA including the spectrofluorimetric [28], spectrophotometric [29], high-performance liquid chromatography [30], fluorescent analysis [31]. But all of these methods require expensive laboratory conditions and sample pretreatments. The electrochemical determination is well-thought-out to the simple and effective determination of the ASA concentration. In the present work, we have reported the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated copper oxide (N-CQD/Cu2O) composite in an in-situ electrochemical method.

Electrochemical preparation method offers preferred morphology and crystal phases for desired applications. N-CQD precisely deposits on the low index {1 1 1} copper crystal plane through lattice matching and also enhances the stability of the Cu2O [35]. In general, nano-crystal with high-index facets is primarily applicable to the high catalytic activity but leads to the probable structural instability, whereas low-index planes {1 1 1} provide excellent electrochemical behavior and moderate stability [36,37]. By keeping the above important phenomena in mind, we are cautious in the synthesis of the low-index plane of Cu2O. Initially, copper micro-cubes are deposited over the glassy carbon electrode using an amperometric method. Then, the deposited copper micro-cubes are electrochemically converted into N-CQD decorated Cu2O. Further, we prepared the electrochemical sensor for the detection of the non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug in the berries based on the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE. The N-CQD/Cu2O modified electrode GCE has shown good electrochemical oxidation of ASA when compared with N-CQD and Cu2O modified GCEs. The obtained results reveal that the composite is highly sensitive and stable which can be credited to the low-index plane {1 1 1} of Cu2O and synergistic effect of the N-CQD.

Copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 99.5%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98.0%), glucose (C6H12O6, 99.5%) were purchased from the SRL Chemicals, India. Acetylsalicylic acid (C9H8O4, 99.0%), citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99.5%) and ethanol (C2H5OH, 95.0%) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, India. All the chemicals were of analytical grade quality and used as per received condition without further purification. The stock solution of ASA (10 mM) was prepared using 0.01 M NaOH and all remaining solutions were made-up of using 18 MΩ De-ionized water.

The synthesis method for N-CQD was adopted as previously reported in the literature with small modifications [38]. Briefly, 3 g of citric acid and 2.6 mL of ethylenediamine was dissolved in 40 mL of DI water and stirred for 10 min to get a homogenous solution, it was kept for 10 min in a domestic microwave oven (700 W) until the reddish brown suspension was obtained and the suspension was dissolved in 100 mL of DI water. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large particles. The remaining solution was filtered using 0.22 µm syringe filter to obtain the N-CQD solution.

Cu MCs were deposited on the surface of GCE using the amperometric technique with few modifications as already reported [39]. Initially, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (10 mM, 5 mL) was dissolved in DI water and then connected with three electrode systems. The reduction potential and deposition time were fixed at −0.6 V and 180 s, respectively. After the deposition time, the reddish layer was formed on the surface of the GCE.

Cu MCs were immersed in a solution containing 0.1 M NaOH and 0.5 mL of N-CQD. For the deposition of N-CQD on the surface of Cu MCs, CV was scanned between −0.5 V and +0.3 V for 40 cycles as shown in Fig. 1 . During the scanning, Cu MCs were converted into Cu2O and the deposition of N-CQD also occurred simultaneously to achieve the N-CQD/Cu2O composite with micro-leaves structure. The obtained film on GCE is known as N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE. For a comparison, Cu2O/GCE was prepared as per the above procedure, with the exception of N-CQD which gives rise to micro-flower structure. The overall preparation procedure is given in Scheme 1 .

The crystallinity of the composite was analyzed using powder X-ray diffraction method (XPERT-PRO; PANalytical B.V., Netherlands). Fourier transform Infra-red (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker IFS28 spectrometer) was used to record the FT-IR spectra using KBr pellets in a room temperature condition. The surface morphology of the as-prepared composite was imaged using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (AU–Quanta450FEG). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was recorded to recognize the physical-electrochemical property of the modified and unmodified electrodes. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out with an electrochemical analyzer CHI 1205a and 900 CH Instruments. A three-electrode system was adopted for the experiments in 10 mL single comportment voltammetric cell. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (surface area 0.0721 cm2) was used as a working electrode with and without modification; an Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and a platinum wire was performed as the counter electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was examined in 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte solution for the preliminary studies on the electrochemical behavior of ASA. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the improvement of electroanalytical methodology and the sensitive determination of anti-inflammatory drug in berries. In all the electrochemical studies inert gas (N2) conditions were maintained.

Fig. 2 A represents the XRD pattern of the Cu2O and N-CQD/Cu2O composite. Fig. S1A shows a broad peak of 2θ range from 20° to 30° is assigned to N-CQD that resembles the (0 0 2) plan of the graphene [40]. The XRD patterns of electrochemically deposited Cu2O and N-CQD/Cu2O composite were demonstrated with peak positions of (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) in Fig. 2A. The obtained results were well matched with the cubic Cu2O structure (JCPDS #050667) [38]. However, the characteristic peak of carbon material (peak between 2θ = 20° to 30°) is detected for the N-CQD/Cu2O composite. This data confirms the existence of N-CQD in N-CQD/Cu2O composite and do not disturb the crystallinity of the Cu2O. At the same time, impurity peaks such as Cu(OH)2 or CuO do not appear in both Cu2O and N-CQD/Cu2O composite which suggests the phase purity of the material. FT-IR spectrum of N-CQD (Fig. S1B) represents the typical merged peaks at 3224 cm-1 and 3355 cm-1, it indicates the NH and OH functional groups stretching vibration respectively. A weak absorption peak around at 1794 cm−1 is originated from the carbonyl (CO) group. A sharp peak centered at 1637 cm-1 is due to the in-plane stretching of carbon–carbon double bond (CC) of the graphitic plane of N-CQD and the small hump at 1288 cm−1 is for vibration of COC bond. The FT-IR spectra of Cu2O and N-CQD/Cu2O are shown in Fig. 2B, a sharp and intense peak at 627 cm−1 is mainly attributed to the stretching of CuO the presence of N-CQD in N-CQD/Cu2O composite. bond and the remaining peaks are almost similar but the stretching frequency intensity of the composite is slightly greater than Cu2O. This is due to the peaks of N-CQD at 3355 cm−1, 3224 cm−1, 1637 cm−1 and 1288 cm−1 are disappeared in N-CQD/Cu2O composite and specify that N-CQD interacts with Cu2O through the OH, NH, CO and COC functional groups [41].

The FE-SEM images of the Cu MCs, Cu2O and N-CQD/Cu2O are shown in the Fig. 3 (A–I). Amperometric deposition of copper is resulted in the well-defined Cu MCs, while the Cu MCs electrochemically scanned in 0.1 M NaOH, all the MCs are converted into a Cu2O with the flower-like structure. The TEM images of N-CQD are displayed in Fig. S2. However, the introduction of N-CQD produces the leaves-like structure of Cu2O with some agglomeration. This indicates N-CQD precisely deposit on the low index {1 1 1} copper crystal plane.

To study the electrochemical kinetics at the interfaces between the electrode and electrolyte, we have done the EIS. Fig. 4 (A&B) displays the characteristic Nyquist plot and a corresponding circuit of N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE, N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE and bare GCE for the real factor (Z') vs. imaginary factor (Z“). These results are helpful in calculating the charge transfer resistance (Rct ) between the electrode and electrolyte interfaces, solution resistance (Rs ) and diffusion coefficient. In general, low Rct value implies that the electrode/electrolyte interface possess greater electron charge transfer between them. The Rct value is directly related to the range of the EIS semi-circle. The EIS spectra are recorded in 0.1 M KCl solution that contains 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as the working electrolyte. The diameter of the semi-circle of the composite is lower than the other modified GCEs. The results state that N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE shows very low Rct value which is well efficient for the electrochemical oxidation of ASA. The low resistance of the composite could be attributed to the presence of N-CQD in the composite and it is responsible for the higher electrical conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of ASA at different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of ASA on different modified electrodes is examined by CV. Fig. 5 shows the electrochemical response of ASA on N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE, Cu2O/GCE, N-CQD/GCE and bare GCE with the existence of 200 µM ASA containing 0.1 M NaOH solution at a scanning rate 50 mV s−1. Further, it exhibits the higher and well-defined oxidation peak of ASA. The composite N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE gave a good electrochemical oxidation current (Ipa = 34.27 µA, Epa = 0.41 V) than Cu2O/GCE (Ipa = 28.25 µA, Epa = 0.41 V), N-CQD/GCE (Ipa = 24.16 µA, Epa = 0.49 V) and bare GCE. In addition, no reduction peak was observed. It which indicates that the ASA oxidation is an irreversible process. ASA oxidation peak in both N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE and Cu2O/GCE is centered at 0.41 V but N-CQD/GCE gives a 0.08 V shifted oxidation peak at 0.49 V. There was a negligible amount of oxidation only observed in bare GCE. The N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE gave the current response 6 µA greater than Cu2O/GCE, and 10 µA greater than N-CQD/GCE. It is expected that the presence of different functional groups in N-CQD/Cu2O composite readily interact with ASA through hydrogen bonding. Further, the presence of Cu2O in the composite also catalyzes the oxidation of ASA. Thus, catalytic oxidation is done effectively than the other modified electrodes. Moreover, the enhanced conductivity and fast electron transfer of the composite (Fig. 4A) is also responsible for the effective oxidation of ASA. The obtained results are higher than the available reports in the literature [42,43]. Hence, N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE is suitable while comparing to other modified electrodes for the further electrochemical studies of ASA. The overall oxidation mechanism of ASA is shown in Scheme 2 .

The CV response of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE in 0.1 M NaOH, containing different concentrations of ASA at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1 is shown in Fig. 6 A. In the absence of ASA (a), the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE did not give any significant peak; in the meantime, a distinct oxidation peak was observed in the presence of 100 µM ASA. Besides, an anodic peak current has increased with the ASA concentration from 100 to 500 µM (b-f). The obtained results exhibited the excellent electro-oxidation performance of ASA at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE and it can be applicable for sensitive detection of ASA. Fig. 6B displays the linear plot between oxidation peak current and concentration of ASA with the linear regression equation Epa (V) = 22.238x +7.0616, R2 = 0.9963.

Fig. 7 A displays the different scanning rate effect of electro-oxidation response on N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE in 0.1 M NaOH to determine the presence of ASA. The oxidation current that obtained directly related to the diffusion process of the ASA on the surface of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE regarding the applied scanning rate. From the CV Fig. 7A, it is clearly observed that with increment in scanning rate from 20 to 200 mV s−1 the oxidation current also increases at the existence of 200 µM ASA. The anodic oxidation peak is lifted towards the positive potential side due to the significant stability of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE. The linearity between the square root of scanning rate and anodic peak current is plotted (Fig. 7B) which represent the oxidation of ASA on N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE, it is predominantly controlled by the diffusion electron transfer method. From the experimental results, we conclude that the obtained CV peaks are mainly due to the oxidation of ASA proceeding at the surface of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE. The resulted linear regression equation is Epa (V) = 2.1646x +3.0193 and correlation coefficient is R2 = 0.9918.

The electro-oxidation of ASA has been examined at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE using DPV. The DPV is a superior sensitive technique for the electro-oxidation of ASA than CV. The DPV response of the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE with various concentration of ASA 1–907 µM containing 0.1 M NaOH is analyzed. A linear relationship between ASA concentration and peak current (Ipa) is plotted and its linear regression value and correlation coefficient are y = 0.0077x + 2.2584 and R2 = 0.9915, respectively. From the slope of the calibration plot as shown in Fig. 8 the sensitivity is calculated. Thus, the calculated limit of detection, sensitivity and linear range are 0.002 µM, 21.87 µA µM−1 cm−2 and 1–907 µM respectively. It is found that the proposed N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE based sensing protocol exhibits higher sensitivity and lower detection limit when compared to the previously reported methods and given in Table S1.

The interference study of the proposed sensor was examined by DPV in the occurrence of 10-fold excess concentration of acetaminophen (AC), uric acid (UA), dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), sucrose (SU), fructose (FR) and lactose (Lac) and the results are shown in Fig. 9 . There was no response for sucrose, fructose, lactose at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE; while DA, glucose, UA, AA, and AC showed a small response. UA, AA, glucose, and AC did not disturb the oxidation peak potential of ASA. The found results clearly indicate that the interference of common interfering molecules was insignificant for the sensitive detection of ASA at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE and also suggested the decent selectivity of the developed sensor.

The accumulation time for the oxidation of ASA was recorded in CV at N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE in the presence of 200 µM ASA in 0.1 M NaOH with the scanning rate of 50 mV s−1 as shown in Fig. 10 A. The CV was recorded for the different accumulation time ranged from 0 to 300 s, however at 120 s greater the oxidation peak current resulted. Hence, the developed sensor was optimized with a suitable accumulation time of 120 s, for the effective oxidation of the ASA. In order to study the reproducibility of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE, five different N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE electrodes were prepared and analyzed for the detection of 200 µM ASA in 0.1 M NaOH at a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1 by CV and shown in Fig. 10B. The outcomes reveal that the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE possesses good reproducibility to sense the ASA. The CV response of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE for 50 continues cycles with the presence of 200 µM ASA was analyzed and pictorially represented in Fig. 10C. The result showed the long-term stability of the modified GCE. The storage stability of N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE was honorable by storing it at room temperature in an air atmosphere for one month. The N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE was lost 2.3% of its original response after one-month storage.

The developed ASA sensor was tested using the real food samples such as Rubus (blackberry), Rubus idaeus (raspberry) and Oxycoccus (cranberry) fruit samples purchased from a local market in Taipei, Taiwan. The fruit sample juices were extracted using water in the mortar. Then each pretreated extract was added (spiked method) to 10 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution separately. DPV experiment was carried out and the obtained DPV results (Fig. 11 (A-F)) undoubtedly establish that the N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE is extremely favorable for real sample analysis and the obtained data are given in Table S2.

In this work, we have prepared the N-CQD/Cu2O composite successfully by simple electrochemical deposition method without using any surfactants or templates at room-temperature. N-CQDs precisely deposited on the low index {1 1 1} copper crystal plane. The prepared N-CQD/Cu2O composite is fabricated for the ASA sensing and showed the enhanced electrochemical response than N-CQD and Cu2O. A DPV based sensing platform for ASA was developed with N-CQD/Cu2O/GCE which exhibits lower detection limit, high sensitivity, and good linear range. The synergistic effect of the N-CQD on low-index plane {1 1 1} of Cu2O was not only improved the stability of the Cu2O but also accredited for the enhancement of fast electron transfer, sensitivity, and electro-chemical stability when compared to the other reported detection protocols. Furthermore, the developed sensor had good selectivity, reproducibility, accumulation time and operational stability. In the view of future work, all these beneficial features can make the proposed sensor which is highly applicable in food and medical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank for the financial support of the UGC – BSR (25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7 – 88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship, India and The Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 106-2113-M-027-003). We would like to thank the University Science Instrumentation Centre (USIC), Alagappa University, Karaikudi, India for the XRD facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.03.095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: fulleretic materials: buckyball-and buckybowl-based crystalline frameworks
